title: Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO / BRVO)
oneLiner: A sudden, painless, unilateral vision loss from venous thrombotic occlusion at the retina, most common in elderly hypertensives – miss it and risk neovascular glaucoma.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 67-year-old man with known hypertension presents to the eye clinic reporting “a curtain fell over my right eye” this morning – painless, blurred vision in the lower half of his visual field. He denies headache, jaw claudication, or scalp tenderness. Visual acuity is 20/80. On fundoscopy, the right retina shows a wedge of flame‑shaped and dot-blot hemorrhages occupying the inferotemporal quadrant, with a few cotton-wool spots. Intraocular pressure is normal. The left eye is unremarkable.

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Age >60 + hypertension** → classic background risk (arteriosclerosis at arteriovenous crossing)
- **Sudden, painless, unilateral** → vascular event, not inflammatory (optic neuritis) or angle-closure glaucoma
- **“Curtain” or sector vision loss** → BRVO – occlusion affecting a branch, not the whole retina
- **Flame/dot-blot hemorrhages in a wedge** → venous congestion and capillary rupture distal to occlusion
- **Cotton-wool spots** → focal ischemia (nerve fiber layer infarcts)
- **Normal IOP** → rules out acute glaucoma (which has high IOP, red eye)
- **No headache or scalp tenderness** → giant cell arteritis (GCA) unlikely (though always check in elderly)

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
1. **Younger patient (30–40s) with no vascular risk factors** – think thrombophilia (antiphospholipid syndrome, factor V Leiden) or oral contraceptive use. Bilateral or recurrent RVO should raise suspicion for systemic hypercoagulable state.
2. **Painful red eye with blurred vision** – actually neovascular glaucoma from ischemic CRVO (rubeosis iridis, high IOP). The pain misleads toward angle closure; look for iris neovascularisation and rule out RVO as cause.
3. **Gradual vision decline over days** – unusual but possible in CRVO with progressive macular edema. Patient may not recall exact onset, mimicking diabetic retinopathy or uveitis. Fundoscopy will show the “blood and thunder” appearance scattered diffusely.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition:**
Retinal vein occlusion (RVO) is a thrombotic obstruction of the central retinal vein (CRVO) or one of its branches (BRVO), leading to venous stasis, capillary rupture, retinal ischemia, and macular edema. The blockage typically occurs at a site of arteriovenous crossing where a thickened artery compresses the vein (BRVO) or at the lamina cribrosa (CRVO) where the central retinal artery and vein share a sheath.

**Why It Matters:**
- **Common:** After diabetic retinopathy, RVO is the second most common retinal vascular cause of vision loss. Prevalence ~0.5–1% in those >40 years.
- **High stakes:** Untreated macular edema causes irreversible central vision loss. Ischemic forms can trigger neovascular glaucoma, a blinding, painful complication requiring emergency panretinal photocoagulation (PRP).
- **Pitfall:** It is easy to mislabel as “benign” vitreous hemorrhage or diabetic retinopathy (which is bilateral). Unilateral sudden painless vision loss in an older adult should always prompt urgent fundoscopy and ophthalmology referral.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story:**
The retinal vein system is low-pressure and thin-walled. In BRVO, the occlusion occurs at an arteriovenous crossing where a hypertensive arteriole crosses over the vein and compresses it – the arteriole is stiff due to atherosclerosis, pinching the vein. In CRVO, the blockage happens at the lamina cribrosa, where the central retinal artery and vein are bound together; arterial wall thickening from hypertension or diabetes narrows the venous lumen, promoting thrombosis.

**Mechanism -> Sign:**
1. **Venous obstruction** → increased venous pressure → dilation and tortuosity of retinal veins (visible on fundoscopy).
2. **Capillary bed back-pressure** → rupture of capillaries → flame-shaped hemorrhages (superficial nerve fiber layer) and dot-blot hemorrhages (deeper layers). In CRVO, these are spread diffusely – “blood and thunder” – because entire retina is affected. In BRVO, hemorrhages are confined to a quadrant.
3. **Ischemia from impaired perfusion** → cotton-wool spots (nerve fiber layer infarcts) and capillary non-perfusion on fluorescein angiography (FFA). The larger the area of non-perfusion, the higher the risk of neovascularisation.
4. **Macular edema** arises from breakdown of the blood-retinal barrier (secondary to ischemia and elevated VEGF) – fluid accumulates in the fovea, causing central blurring.
5. **Neovascularisation** (late, if ischemic) – retinal ischemia upregulates VEGF, promoting abnormal vessel growth on the retina, disc, or iris (rubeosis iridis). These vessels are fragile, leak, and can cause vitreous hemorrhage or obstruct aqueous outflow, leading to neovascular glaucoma.

**Connection to Pattern Recognition:**
- “Painless” because retinal tissue has no pain fibers; pain only arises when neovascular glaucoma elevates IOP (late).
- “Wedge shape” in BRVO corresponds to the drainage territory of the occluded branch; the most common is superotemporal (80%) because that crossing is more vulnerable.
- “Blood and thunder” in CRVO reflects diffuse deep hemorrhages – a hallmark finding.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic / Vascular (most common)** | Hypertension, atherosclerosis, older age | Elderly patient with known HTN, sudden vision loss |
| | Diabetes mellitus | Concomitant diabetic retinopathy; bilateral? |
| | Hyperlipidemia, smoking | Modifiable risk factors present |
| **Hypercoagulable / Hematologic** | Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, antiphospholipid syndrome | Young patient (<50), recurrent RVO, personal/family DVT history |
| | Hyperhomocysteinemia | Low B12/folate, methotrexate use |
| | Polycythemia, thrombocytosis | High hemoglobin/platelet count |
| **Inflammatory / Vasculitis** | Giant cell arteritis (GCA) | Age >60, headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, elevated ESR/CRP. Can cause CRVO from arteritic occlusion |
| | Sarcoidosis, Behçet’s | Systemic inflammatory features, uveitis masquerading as RVO |
| **Mechanical / Compressive** | Orbital mass, thyroid eye disease, optic nerve drusen (rare) | Proptosis, disc edema, visual field defect extending beyond one quadrant |
| **Iatrogenic / Other** | Oral contraceptives, HRT | Young woman on OCP; unilateral RVO |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic arteriosclerosis / hypertension (accounts for >50% of cases in older adults). Always start with blood pressure measurement.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:**
RVO is primarily a **clinical diagnosis** on fundoscopy. There is no formal score. The key is to confirm the type (CRVO vs BRVO) and assess for ischemia.

**Tests and Interpretation:**

| Investigation | What It Shows | Why Order | How to Interpret |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Fundoscopy** | Flame/dot-blot hemorrhages, venous dilation, cotton-wool spots | Immediate diagnosis | CRVO: diffuse hemorrhages in all four quadrants; BRVO: sectoral wedge |
| **Fluorescein angiography (FFA)** | Capillary non-perfusion, macular edema, neovascularisation | Assess ischemia (risk of neovascularisation) and guide treatment | >10 disc areas of non-perfusion = ischemic CRVO (high risk for neovascular glaucoma) |
| **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** | Macular thickness, cystoid spaces | Quantify macular edema; monitor response to anti-VEGF | Central subfield thickness >300 µm suggests significant edema |
| **Blood pressure (clinic + home)** | Systolic/diastolic readings | Identify and treat underlying hypertension | >140/90 mmHg → initiate antihypertensives |
| **HbA1c, fasting glucose** | Diabetes status | Rule out diabetic retinopathy (bilateral) and manage risk | >6.5% indicates diabetes |
| **Lipid profile** | Cholesterol, triglycerides | Address hyperlipidemia risk | LDL >3.0 mmol/L → consider statin |
| **ESR, CRP** (if age >50, especially >70) | Elevated in giant cell arteritis | Rule out arteritic cause (needs urgent steroids) | ESR >50 mm/h + typical symptoms = start prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day |
| **Thrombophilia screen** (if young, bilateral, recurrent) | Factor V Leiden, APCR, lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin | Identify hypercoagulable state | Positive lupus anticoagulant → antiphospholipid syndrome; manage with anticoagulation if other thromboses |

**Single finding that confirms RVO:** Fundoscopic appearance of flame/dot-blot hemorrhages in a sector (BRVO) or diffuse (CRVO) distribution, **unilateral**, in a patient with vascular risk factors.

**Rule-out findings:**
- Bilateral symmetric hemorrhages → diabetic retinopathy (look for hard exudates, microaneurysms).
- Optic disc edema with preserved venous caliber → anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) – no hemorrhages.
- Red eye with high IOP → acute angle closure.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour) – Emergency Department / Clinic:**
- **No specific acute treatment for the occlusion itself.** RVO is not an emergency like acute stroke – you have time.
- **If giant cell arteritis suspected** (headache, jaw claudication, elevated ESR) – start **prednisolone 1 mg/kg oral** (e.g., 60 mg OD) immediately to prevent bilateral optic nerve damage. Do not wait for biopsy.
- **Measure visual acuity** and **check IOP** – if IOP >30 mmHg with rubeosis (neovascular glaucoma) – give **topical timolol 0.5% one drop** and call ophthalmology for PRP.
- **Control severe hypertension** – if BP >180/110, treat gradually with oral labetalol or amlodipine (avoid rapid drop which may worsen ocular perfusion).

**Ward / Clinic (24–72 Hours) – Specialty Initiation:**
- **Perform FFA and OCT** to classify as ischemic vs non-ischemic, and assess macular edema.
- **Macular edema with vision <20/40 (or any foveal edema)** : first-line treatment is **intravitreal anti-VEGF**.
  - **Ranibizumab 0.5 mg (0.05 mL)** intravitreal, once monthly for 3 months, then PRN.
  - Or **aflibercept 2 mg (0.05 mL)** similarly.
  - Dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex) is an alternative if anti-VEGF fails or patient prefers fewer injections (lasts 3–4 months). Risk of cataract and IOP rise.
- **Ischemic CRVO (≥10 disc areas of non-perfusion)** : arrange **panretinal photocoagulation (PRP)** within 2–4 weeks to prevent neovascularisation. Urgent PRP if neo vessels already present on iris or disc.
- **Systemic risk factor optimisation:**
  - Start **aspirin 75 mg OD** (no evidence it reverses occlusion, but reduces secondary cardiovascular risk). **Do not** start anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) unless there is proven thromboembolism or hypercoagulable state – risk of worsening hemorrhage is theoretical but real.
  - Manage hypertension to target <140/90.
  - Statin (atorvastatin 20 mg OD) if LDL >3.0.
  - Control diabetes: HbA1c target <7%.

**Long-Term (Months to Years):**
- **Monthly monitoring** for recurrence of macular edema – OCT-guided treatment. Anti-VEGF injections continue PRN (typical 5–8 in first year).
- **Observe for neovascular complications** – examine iris quarterly for rubeosis.
- **Consider treatment for macular edema that persists beyond 12 months** – laser grid therapy for BRVO is now rarely used (anti-VEGF superior).
- **Discontinue aspirin if neovascular glaucoma develops?** No – continue aspirin for systemic prevention; the risk of vitreous hemorrhage from neo vessels is not increased by aspirin. However, if massive vitreous hemorrhage occurs, ophthalmology may decide to pause.

**Common Pitfalls – What NOT to Do:**
- ❌ **Do not start oral steroids for “retinal vein inflammation”** – RVO is thrombotic, not vasculitic (unless GCA). Steroids increase IOP and don’t help.
- ❌ **Do not start anticoagulation empirically** – no benefit and may increase hemorrhagic transformation.
- ❌ **Do not diagnose “vitreous hemorrhage” from fundoscopy alone without dilating the pupil** – you’ll miss the underlying RVO.
- ❌ **Do not ignore the contralateral eye** – risk of bilateral involvement is low (<5%) but if bilateral, investigate for systemic disease (thrombophilia, vasculitis).
- ❌ **Do not reassure a patient with mild macular edema that it will resolve on its own** – even mild edema can cause permanent photoreceptor damage; treat early.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Retinal Vein Occlusion (BRVO/CRVO) | Acute Angle Closure Glaucoma | Optic Neuritis | Amaurosis Fugax (TIA) | Diabetic Retinopathy (Proliferative) |
|---------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Painless (unless neovascular glaucoma) | Severe eye pain, headache, nausea | Pain on eye movement (retro-orbital) | Painless | Painless |
| **Onset** | Sudden | Sudden | Subacute (hours–days) | Transient (seconds–minutes) | Gradual, bilateral |
| **Vision** | Blurred/central scotoma; sector defect in BRVO | Marked reduction, halos | Central scotoma, reduced colour | “Curtain” over vision, complete recovery | Floaters, vision loss if vitreous hemorrhage |
| **Exam** | Flame/dot-blot hemorrhages, venous dilation, cotton-wool spots | Corneal edema, mid-dilated fixed pupil, high IOP | Afferent pupillary defect, optic disc edema (normal retina) | Normal between episodes | Microaneurysms, hard exudates, neovascularisation (bilateral) |
| **Key Differentiator** | Unilateral hemorrhages in venous territory | Red eye, high IOP, fixed pupil | Painful eye movement, RAPD | Transient (minutes), normal retina | Bilateral, symmetrical, microaneurysms |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Macular edema** (most common, early): presents as persistent blurred central vision. If untreated for >3–6 months, photoreceptor damage becomes irreversible. **What to do:** initiate intravitreal anti-VEGF promptly; re-evaluate monthly.
- **Neovascular glaucoma** (dreaded complication, ischemic CRVO): presents 2–4 months after occlusion. Iris neovascularisation (rubeosis iridis) blocks the trabecular meshwork, causing sudden severe eye pain, redness, high IOP, corneal edema, vision loss to hand motions. **What to do:** emergency PRP, topical timolol, IOP-lowering agents; often requires glaucoma surgery (trabeculectomy, tube shunt). Vision prognosis is poor.
- **Vitreous hemorrhage** (from neo vessels): sudden floaters, “cobwebs” or “blobs” in vision. Fundoscopy may be obscured. **What to do:** urgent B-scan ultrasound to rule out retinal detachment; refer for PRP if neo vessels seen.
- **Retinal detachment** (rare, from neo vessels or if concurrent vitreous hemorrhage): flashing lights, curtain. **What to do:** same-day ophthalmology – vitrectomy may be needed.
- **Chronic cystoid macular edema** (late) – if repeated anti-VEGF injections fail, consider steroid implant (Ozurdex) but monitor for glaucoma and cataract.

**Late-presentation patterns for exams:**
- “A 70-year-old presents with a painful red eye 3 months after sudden vision loss; IOP is 50 mmHg, iris vessels are visible.” → Neovascular glaucoma from missed ischemic CRVO.
- “Patient with BRVO noted 6 months ago who now has dense floaters in the same eye.” → Vitreous hemorrhage from retinal neovascularisation.
- “Visual acuity 20/200 but fundus view is hazy due to rubeosis and corneal edema.” → End-stage neovascular glaucoma.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic – RVO Risk Factors:**  
**“A**ge / **H**ypertension / **D**iabetes / **H**yperlipidemia / **T**hrombophilia”  
→ Remember: **A-HD-HT** (say “A Hot Dog Hypertension” – silly but sticks)

**Mnemonic – BRVO vs CRVO Presentation:**  
**“B**ranch = **B**oxer’s injury (sectoral, like a punch to one quadrant)  
**C**entral = **C**lueless (whole retina involved, blood and thunder)”

**High-Yield Pearls:**
1. **“Blood and thunder” is pathognomonic for CRVO** – if you see it, stop looking for mimics.
2. **Check the iris BEFORE dilating** – rubeosis iridis on the iris surface (small, irregular vessels) is a sign of ischemic CRVO. If present, urgent PRP.
3. **BRVO most often involves the superotemporal quadrant** – quiz question loves this.
4. **A sudden vision loss in a young woman on OCP + dot-blot hemorrhages** → immediate thrombophilia workup.
5. **If ESR is elevated in an older patient with CRVO, start steroids before biopsy** – GCA can cause bilateral sequential RVO.
6. **Monthly anti-VEGF injections are the standard of care for macular edema** – do not rely on observation alone.
7. **Do not anticoagulate for RVO** unless there is proven DVT/PE or antiphospholipid syndrome with other thromboses. Aspirin 75 mg is for secondary cardiovascular prevention only.
8. **Bilateral RVO is rare (<5%) and should always trigger systemic investigation** – vasculitis (GCA, SLE), thrombophilia, or severe hypertension.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes:**
- Mistaking RVO for diabetic retinopathy (bilateral vs unilateral)
- Missing rubeosis iridis because pupil not examined before dilation
- Delaying anti-VEGF because “edema might resolve”
- Ordering routine thrombophilia screen for every 75-year-old (wasteful – only in young or recurrent cases)
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Situation | Action | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Suspected giant cell arteritis** (headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, elevated ESR/CRP) | Urgent ophthalmology + start prednisolone 1 mg/kg PO | Same-day review; do not wait for biopsy result |
| **Neovascular glaucoma** (pain, red eye, high IOP, iris rubeosis) | Emergency ophthalmology referral for PRP and IOP control | IOP >30 mmHg; vision suddenly drops |
| **Sudden vision loss <20/200 with RAPD** (relative afferent pupillary defect) | Same-day ophthalmology (could be ischemic CRVO or other optic neuropathy) | RAPD present |
| **NEWS2 ≥5** (if patient admitted with hypertension or other medical problem) | Escalate to medical team; consider hypertensive emergency | Systolic >180 + end-organ damage |
| **Vitreous hemorrhage + inability to see fundus** | Urgent B-scan ultrasound by ophthalmology (rule out detachment) | Patient reports “curtain” or flashing lights |

**General rule:** Any sudden, painless, unilateral vision loss in an elderly person requires same-day fundoscopy and ophthalmology referral. Do not discharge without documentation of IOP and iris examination.

rawMarkdown:
## Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO / BRVO)
**One-liner:** A sudden, painless, unilateral vision loss from venous thrombotic occlusion at the retina, most common in elderly hypertensives – miss it and risk neovascular glaucoma.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 67-year-old man with known hypertension presents to the eye clinic reporting “a curtain fell over my right eye” this morning – painless, blurred vision in the lower half of his visual field. He denies headache, jaw claudication, or scalp tenderness. Visual acuity is 20/80. On fundoscopy, the right retina shows a wedge of flame‑shaped and dot-blot hemorrhages occupying the inferotemporal quadrant, with a few cotton-wool spots. Intraocular pressure is normal. The left eye is unremarkable.

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Age >60 + hypertension** → classic background risk (arteriosclerosis at arteriovenous crossing)
- **Sudden, painless, unilateral** → vascular event, not inflammatory (optic neuritis) or angle-closure glaucoma
- **“Curtain” or sector vision loss** → BRVO – occlusion affecting a branch, not the whole retina
- **Flame/dot-blot hemorrhages in a wedge** → venous congestion and capillary rupture distal to occlusion
- **Cotton-wool spots** → focal ischemia (nerve fiber layer infarcts)
- **Normal IOP** → rules out acute glaucoma (which has high IOP, red eye)
- **No headache or scalp tenderness** → giant cell arteritis (GCA) unlikely (though always check in elderly)

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
1. **Younger patient (30–40s) with no vascular risk factors** – think thrombophilia (antiphospholipid syndrome, factor V Leiden) or oral contraceptive use. Bilateral or recurrent RVO should raise suspicion for systemic hypercoagulable state.
2. **Painful red eye with blurred vision** – actually neovascular glaucoma from ischemic CRVO (rubeosis iridis, high IOP). The pain misleads toward angle closure; look for iris neovascularisation and rule out RVO as cause.
3. **Gradual vision decline over days** – unusual but possible in CRVO with progressive macular edema. Patient may not recall exact onset, mimicking diabetic retinopathy or uveitis. Fundoscopy will show the “blood and thunder” appearance scattered diffusely.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Definition:**
Retinal vein occlusion (RVO) is a thrombotic obstruction of the central retinal vein (CRVO) or one of its branches (BRVO), leading to venous stasis, capillary rupture, retinal ischemia, and macular edema. The blockage typically occurs at a site of arteriovenous crossing where a thickened artery compresses the vein (BRVO) or at the lamina cribrosa (CRVO) where the central retinal artery and vein share a sheath.

**Why It Matters:**
- **Common:** After diabetic retinopathy, RVO is the second most common retinal vascular cause of vision loss. Prevalence ~0.5–1% in those >40 years.
- **High stakes:** Untreated macular edema causes irreversible central vision loss. Ischemic forms can trigger neovascular glaucoma, a blinding, painful complication requiring emergency panretinal photocoagulation (PRP).
- **Pitfall:** It is easy to mislabel as “benign” vitreous hemorrhage or diabetic retinopathy (which is bilateral). Unilateral sudden painless vision loss in an older adult should always prompt urgent fundoscopy and ophthalmology referral.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story:**
The retinal vein system is low-pressure and thin-walled. In BRVO, the occlusion occurs at an arteriovenous crossing where a hypertensive arteriole crosses over the vein and compresses it – the arteriole is stiff due to atherosclerosis, pinching the vein. In CRVO, the blockage happens at the lamina cribrosa, where the central retinal artery and vein are bound together; arterial wall thickening from hypertension or diabetes narrows the venous lumen, promoting thrombosis.

**Mechanism -> Sign:**
1. **Venous obstruction** → increased venous pressure → dilation and tortuosity of retinal veins (visible on fundoscopy).
2. **Capillary bed back-pressure** → rupture of capillaries → flame-shaped hemorrhages (superficial nerve fiber layer) and dot-blot hemorrhages (deeper layers). In CRVO, these are spread diffusely – “blood and thunder” – because entire retina is affected. In BRVO, hemorrhages are confined to a quadrant.
3. **Ischemia from impaired perfusion** → cotton-wool spots (nerve fiber layer infarcts) and capillary non-perfusion on fluorescein angiography (FFA). The larger the area of non-perfusion, the higher the risk of neovascularisation.
4. **Macular edema** arises from breakdown of the blood-retinal barrier (secondary to ischemia and elevated VEGF) – fluid accumulates in the fovea, causing central blurring.
5. **Neovascularisation** (late, if ischemic) – retinal ischemia upregulates VEGF, promoting abnormal vessel growth on the retina, disc, or iris (rubeosis iridis). These vessels are fragile, leak, and can cause vitreous hemorrhage or obstruct aqueous outflow, leading to neovascular glaucoma.

**Connection to Pattern Recognition:**
- “Painless” because retinal tissue has no pain fibers; pain only arises when neovascular glaucoma elevates IOP (late).
- “Wedge shape” in BRVO corresponds to the drainage territory of the occluded branch; the most common is superotemporal (80%) because that crossing is more vulnerable.
- “Blood and thunder” in CRVO reflects diffuse deep hemorrhages – a hallmark finding.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic / Vascular (most common)** | Hypertension, atherosclerosis, older age | Elderly patient with known HTN, sudden vision loss |
| | Diabetes mellitus | Concomitant diabetic retinopathy; bilateral? |
| | Hyperlipidemia, smoking | Modifiable risk factors present |
| **Hypercoagulable / Hematologic** | Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, antiphospholipid syndrome | Young patient (<50), recurrent RVO, personal/family DVT history |
| | Hyperhomocysteinemia | Low B12/folate, methotrexate use |
| | Polycythemia, thrombocytosis | High hemoglobin/platelet count |
| **Inflammatory / Vasculitis** | Giant cell arteritis (GCA) | Age >60, headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, elevated ESR/CRP. Can cause CRVO from arteritic occlusion |
| | Sarcoidosis, Behçet’s | Systemic inflammatory features, uveitis masquerading as RVO |
| **Mechanical / Compressive** | Orbital mass, thyroid eye disease, optic nerve drusen (rare) | Proptosis, disc edema, visual field defect extending beyond one quadrant |
| **Iatrogenic / Other** | Oral contraceptives, HRT | Young woman on OCP; unilateral RVO |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic arteriosclerosis / hypertension (accounts for >50% of cases in older adults). Always start with blood pressure measurement.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria:**
RVO is primarily a **clinical diagnosis** on fundoscopy. There is no formal score. The key is to confirm the type (CRVO vs BRVO) and assess for ischemia.

**Tests and Interpretation:**

| Investigation | What It Shows | Why Order | How to Interpret |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Fundoscopy** | Flame/dot-blot hemorrhages, venous dilation, cotton-wool spots | Immediate diagnosis | CRVO: diffuse hemorrhages in all four quadrants; BRVO: sectoral wedge |
| **Fluorescein angiography (FFA)** | Capillary non-perfusion, macular edema, neovascularisation | Assess ischemia (risk of neovascularisation) and guide treatment | >10 disc areas of non-perfusion = ischemic CRVO (high risk for neovascular glaucoma) |
| **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** | Macular thickness, cystoid spaces | Quantify macular edema; monitor response to anti-VEGF | Central subfield thickness >300 µm suggests significant edema |
| **Blood pressure (clinic + home)** | Systolic/diastolic readings | Identify and treat underlying hypertension | >140/90 mmHg → initiate antihypertensives |
| **HbA1c, fasting glucose** | Diabetes status | Rule out diabetic retinopathy (bilateral) and manage risk | >6.5% indicates diabetes |
| **Lipid profile** | Cholesterol, triglycerides | Address hyperlipidemia risk | LDL >3.0 mmol/L → consider statin |
| **ESR, CRP** (if age >50, especially >70) | Elevated in giant cell arteritis | Rule out arteritic cause (needs urgent steroids) | ESR >50 mm/h + typical symptoms = start prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day |
| **Thrombophilia screen** (if young, bilateral, recurrent) | Factor V Leiden, APCR, lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin | Identify hypercoagulable state | Positive lupus anticoagulant → antiphospholipid syndrome; manage with anticoagulation if other thromboses |

**Single finding that confirms RVO:** Fundoscopic appearance of flame/dot-blot hemorrhages in a sector (BRVO) or diffuse (CRVO) distribution, **unilateral**, in a patient with vascular risk factors.

**Rule-out findings:**
- Bilateral symmetric hemorrhages → diabetic retinopathy (look for hard exudates, microaneurysms).
- Optic disc edema with preserved venous caliber → anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) – no hemorrhages.
- Red eye with high IOP → acute angle closure.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour) – Emergency Department / Clinic:**
- **No specific acute treatment for the occlusion itself.** RVO is not an emergency like acute stroke – you have time.
- **If giant cell arteritis suspected** (headache, jaw claudication, elevated ESR) – start **prednisolone 1 mg/kg oral** (e.g., 60 mg OD) immediately to prevent bilateral optic nerve damage. Do not wait for biopsy.
- **Measure visual acuity** and **check IOP** – if IOP >30 mmHg with rubeosis (neovascular glaucoma) – give **topical timolol 0.5% one drop** and call ophthalmology for PRP.
- **Control severe hypertension** – if BP >180/110, treat gradually with oral labetalol or amlodipine (avoid rapid drop which may worsen ocular perfusion).

**Ward / Clinic (24–72 Hours) – Specialty Initiation:**
- **Perform FFA and OCT** to classify as ischemic vs non-ischemic, and assess macular edema.
- **Macular edema with vision <20/40 (or any foveal edema)** : first-line treatment is **intravitreal anti-VEGF**.
  - **Ranibizumab 0.5 mg (0.05 mL)** intravitreal, once monthly for 3 months, then PRN.
  - Or **aflibercept 2 mg (0.05 mL)** similarly.
  - Dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex) is an alternative if anti-VEGF fails or patient prefers fewer injections (lasts 3–4 months). Risk of cataract and IOP rise.
- **Ischemic CRVO (≥10 disc areas of non-perfusion)** : arrange **panretinal photocoagulation (PRP)** within 2–4 weeks to prevent neovascularisation. Urgent PRP if neo vessels already present on iris or disc.
- **Systemic risk factor optimisation:**
  - Start **aspirin 75 mg OD** (no evidence it reverses occlusion, but reduces secondary cardiovascular risk). **Do not** start anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) unless there is proven thromboembolism or hypercoagulable state – risk of worsening hemorrhage is theoretical but real.
  - Manage hypertension to target <140/90.
  - Statin (atorvastatin 20 mg OD) if LDL >3.0.
  - Control diabetes: HbA1c target <7%.

**Long-Term (Months to Years):**
- **Monthly monitoring** for recurrence of macular edema – OCT-guided treatment. Anti-VEGF injections continue PRN (typical 5–8 in first year).
- **Observe for neovascular complications** – examine iris quarterly for rubeosis.
- **Consider treatment for macular edema that persists beyond 12 months** – laser grid therapy for BRVO is now rarely used (anti-VEGF superior).
- **Discontinue aspirin if neovascular glaucoma develops?** No – continue aspirin for systemic prevention; the risk of vitreous hemorrhage from neo vessels is not increased by aspirin. However, if massive vitreous hemorrhage occurs, ophthalmology may decide to pause.

**Common Pitfalls – What NOT to Do:**
- ❌ **Do not start oral steroids for “retinal vein inflammation”** – RVO is thrombotic, not vasculitic (unless GCA). Steroids increase IOP and don’t help.
- ❌ **Do not start anticoagulation empirically** – no benefit and may increase hemorrhagic transformation.
- ❌ **Do not diagnose “vitreous hemorrhage” from fundoscopy alone without dilating the pupil** – you’ll miss the underlying RVO.
- ❌ **Do not ignore the contralateral eye** – risk of bilateral involvement is low (<5%) but if bilateral, investigate for systemic disease (thrombophilia, vasculitis).
- ❌ **Do not reassure a patient with mild macular edema that it will resolve on its own** – even mild edema can cause permanent photoreceptor damage; treat early.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Retinal Vein Occlusion (BRVO/CRVO) | Acute Angle Closure Glaucoma | Optic Neuritis | Amaurosis Fugax (TIA) | Diabetic Retinopathy (Proliferative) |
|---------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Painless (unless neovascular glaucoma) | Severe eye pain, headache, nausea | Pain on eye movement (retro-orbital) | Painless | Painless |
| **Onset** | Sudden | Sudden | Subacute (hours–days) | Transient (seconds–minutes) | Gradual, bilateral |
| **Vision** | Blurred/central scotoma; sector defect in BRVO | Marked reduction, halos | Central scotoma, reduced colour | “Curtain” over vision, complete recovery | Floaters, vision loss if vitreous hemorrhage |
| **Exam** | Flame/dot-blot hemorrhages, venous dilation, cotton-wool spots | Corneal edema, mid-dilated fixed pupil, high IOP | Afferent pupillary defect, optic disc edema (normal retina) | Normal between episodes | Microaneurysms, hard exudates, neovascularisation (bilateral) |
| **Key Differentiator** | Unilateral hemorrhages in venous territory | Red eye, high IOP, fixed pupil | Painful eye movement, RAPD | Transient (minutes), normal retina | Bilateral, symmetrical, microaneurysms |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Macular edema** (most common, early): presents as persistent blurred central vision. If untreated for >3–6 months, photoreceptor damage becomes irreversible. **What to do:** initiate intravitreal anti-VEGF promptly; re-evaluate monthly.
- **Neovascular glaucoma** (dreaded complication, ischemic CRVO): presents 2–4 months after occlusion. Iris neovascularisation (rubeosis iridis) blocks the trabecular meshwork, causing sudden severe eye pain, redness, high IOP, corneal edema, vision loss to hand motions. **What to do:** emergency PRP, topical timolol, IOP-lowering agents; often requires glaucoma surgery (trabeculectomy, tube shunt). Vision prognosis is poor.
- **Vitreous hemorrhage** (from neo vessels): sudden floaters, “cobwebs” or “blobs” in vision. Fundoscopy may be obscured. **What to do:** urgent B-scan ultrasound to rule out retinal detachment; refer for PRP if neo vessels seen.
- **Retinal detachment** (rare, from neo vessels or if concurrent vitreous hemorrhage): flashing lights, curtain. **What to do:** same-day ophthalmology – vitrectomy may be needed.
- **Chronic cystoid macular edema** (late) – if repeated anti-VEGF injections fail, consider steroid implant (Ozurdex) but monitor for glaucoma and cataract.

**Late-presentation patterns for exams:**
- “A 70-year-old presents with a painful red eye 3 months after sudden vision loss; IOP is 50 mmHg, iris vessels are visible.” → Neovascular glaucoma from missed ischemic CRVO.
- “Patient with BRVO noted 6 months ago who now has dense floaters in the same eye.” → Vitreous hemorrhage from retinal neovascularisation.
- “Visual acuity 20/200 but fundus view is hazy due to rubeosis and corneal edema.” → End-stage neovascular glaucoma.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic – RVO Risk Factors:**  
**“A**ge / **H**ypertension / **D**iabetes / **H**yperlipidemia / **T**hrombophilia”  
→ Remember: **A-HD-HT** (say “A Hot Dog Hypertension” – silly but sticks)

**Mnemonic – BRVO vs CRVO Presentation:**  
**“B**ranch = **B**oxer’s injury (sectoral, like a punch to one quadrant)  
**C**entral = **C**lueless (whole retina involved, blood and thunder)”

**High-Yield Pearls:**
1. **“Blood and thunder” is pathognomonic for CRVO** – if you see it, stop looking for mimics.
2. **Check the iris BEFORE dilating** – rubeosis iridis on the iris surface (small, irregular vessels) is a sign of ischemic CRVO. If present, urgent PRP.
3. **BRVO most often involves the superotemporal quadrant** – quiz question loves this.
4. **A sudden vision loss in a young woman on OCP + dot-blot hemorrhages** → immediate thrombophilia workup.
5. **If ESR is elevated in an older patient with CRVO, start steroids before biopsy** – GCA can cause bilateral sequential RVO.
6. **Monthly anti-VEGF injections are the standard of care for macular edema** – do not rely on observation alone.
7. **Do not anticoagulate for RVO** unless there is proven DVT/PE or antiphospholipid syndrome with other thromboses. Aspirin 75 mg is for secondary cardiovascular prevention only.
8. **Bilateral RVO is rare (<5%) and should always trigger systemic investigation** – vasculitis (GCA, SLE), thrombophilia, or severe hypertension.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes:**
- Mistaking RVO for diabetic retinopathy (bilateral vs unilateral)
- Missing rubeosis iridis because pupil not examined before dilation
- Delaying anti-VEGF because “edema might resolve”
- Ordering routine thrombophilia screen for every 75-year-old (wasteful – only in young or recurrent cases)

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Situation | Action | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Suspected giant cell arteritis** (headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, elevated ESR/CRP) | Urgent ophthalmology + start prednisolone 1 mg/kg PO | Same-day review; do not wait for biopsy result |
| **Neovascular glaucoma** (pain, red eye, high IOP, iris rubeosis) | Emergency ophthalmology referral for PRP and IOP control | IOP >30 mmHg; vision suddenly drops |
| **Sudden vision loss <20/200 with RAPD** (relative afferent pupillary defect) | Same-day ophthalmology (could be ischemic CRVO or other optic neuropathy) | RAPD present |
| **NEWS2 ≥5** (if patient admitted with hypertension or other medical problem) | Escalate to medical team; consider hypertensive emergency | Systolic >180 + end-organ damage |
| **Vitreous hemorrhage + inability to see fundus** | Urgent B-scan ultrasound by ophthalmology (rule out detachment) | Patient reports “curtain” or flashing lights |

**General rule:** Any sudden, painless, unilateral vision loss in an elderly person requires same-day fundoscopy and ophthalmology referral. Do not discharge without documentation of IOP and iris examination.
